perturbation.original_text: Any disputes, arising out or relating to this Agreement, shall be settled by the ordinary courts in the Inventor's/Disclosing Party's home country and according to the legislation of the Inventor 's/Disclosing Party's home country.
perturbation.changed_text: This agreement does not cover: Any disputes, arising out or relating to this Agreement, shall be settled by the ordinary courts in the Inventor's/Disclosing Party's home country and according to the legislation of the Inventor 's/Disclosing Party's home country.
perturbation.explanation: Moving the dispute resolution clause to the section that describes what the agreement *doesn't* cover obfuscates the method for dispute resolution, creating ambiguity. Dispute resolution should be clear and easily found.
perturbation.contradicted_law: 9 U.S. Code § 2 - Validity, irrevocability, and enforcement of agreements to arbitrate
perturbation.law_explanation: By placing the dispute resolution clause within the exclusions section, the agreement creates uncertainty about the enforceability of the dispute resolution process, contradicting the intent of 9 U.S. Code § 2, which validates and enforces arbitration agreements. If the parties do not understand where to locate the rules for disputes, that will cause an issue.

perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: 9 U.S. Code § 2 - Validity, irrevocability, and enforcement of agreements to arbitrate | U.S. Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Quick search by citation: Title Section Go! 9 U.S. Code § 2 - Validity, irrevocability, and enforcement of agreements to arbitrate U.S. Code Notes prev | next A written provision in any maritime transaction or a contract evidencing a transaction involving commerce to settle by arbitration a controversy thereafter arising out of such contract or transaction, or the refusal to perform the whole or any part thereof, or an agreement in writing to submit to arbitration an existing controversy arising out of such a contract, transaction, or refusal, shall be valid, irrevocable, and enforceable, save upon such grounds as exist at law or in equity for the revocation of any contract or as otherwise provided in chapter 4. (July 30, 1947, ch. 392, 61 Stat. 670 ; Pub. L. 117–90, § 2(b)(1)(A) , Mar. 3, 2022 , 136 Stat. 27 .) Derivation Act Feb. 12, 1925, ch. 213, § 2, 43 Stat. 883 . Editorial Notes Amendments 2022— Pub. L. 117–90 inserted “or as otherwise provided in chapter 4” before period at end. Statutory Notes and Related Subsidiaries Effective Date of 2022 Amendment Amendment by Pub. L. 117–90 applicable with respect to any dispute or claim that arises or accrues on or after Mar. 3, 2022 , see section 3 of Pub. L. 117–90 , set out as an Effective Date note under section 401 of this title . U.S. Code Toolbox Law about... Articles from Wex Table of Popular Names Parallel Table of Authorities How current is this?
perturbation.justification: The changed text moves the dispute resolution clause to a section describing what the agreement *doesn't* cover. This placement creates ambiguity about whether the clause is actually part of the agreement, undermining its enforceability. 9 U.S. Code § 2, as stated in both snippets, concerns the "Validity, irrevocability, and enforcement of agreements to arbitrate." By obscuring the dispute resolution process, the modified contract weakens its enforceability and makes it difficult for parties to find and understand, thus creating a structural flaw and undermining the intent of the law.

perturbation.original_text: If considered necessary or justiﬁable, the Receiving Party shall, before passing on the information to third parties, instruct such third parties about the conﬁdentiality of the Information, and they shall sign a conﬁdentiality and non-disclosure agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: The Receiving Party is held liable to the Inventor/Disclosing Party for damages in the event of breach of this Agreement. If considered necessary or justiﬁable, the Receiving Party shall, before passing on the information to third parties, instruct such third parties about the conﬁdentiality of the Information, and they shall sign a conﬁdentiality and non-disclosure agreement.
perturbation.explanation: Moving this clause to after the liability statement causes there to be an instruction on NDA after liability. This reverses the order it should be in, thus causing a conflict.
perturbation.contradicted_law: 15 U.S. Code § 45 - Unfair methods of competition unlawful; prevention by Commission
perturbation.law_explanation: If parties do not understand the NDA or aren't able to have NDA signed before liability statement, this could be deemed a method of unfair competition. The liability should be known after the person is allowed to have an NDA or non-disclosure agreement signed.

perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: 15 U.S. Code § 45 - Unfair methods of competition unlawful; prevention by Commission | U.S. Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Quick search by citation: Title Section Go! 15 U.S. Code § 45 - Unfair methods of competition unlawful; prevention by Commission U.S. Code Notes Authorities (CFR) prev | next (a) Declaration of unlawfulness; power to prohibit unfair practices; inapplicability to foreign trade (1) Unfair methods of competition in or affecting commerce , and unfair or deceptive acts or practices in or affecting commerce, are hereby declared unlawful. (2) The Commission is hereby empowered and directed to prevent persons, partnerships, or corporations , except banks, savings and loan institutions described in section 57a(f)(3) of this title , Federal credit unions described in section 57a(f)(4) of this title , common carriers subject to the Acts to regulate commerce, air carriers and foreign air carriers subject to part A of subtitle VII of title 49, and persons, partnerships, or corporations insofar as they are subject to the Packers and Stockyards Act, 1921 , as amended [ 7 U.S.C. 181 et seq.], except as provided in section 406(b) of said Act [ 7 U.S.C. 227(b) ], from using unfair methods of competition in or affecting commerce and unfair or deceptive acts or practices in or affecting commerce. (3) This subsection shall not apply to unfair methods of competition involving commerce with foreign nations (other than import commerce ) unless— (A) such methods of competition have a direct, substantial, and reasonably foreseeable effect— (i) on commerce which is not commerce with foreign nations, or on import commerce with foreign nations; or (ii) on export commerce with foreign nations, of a person engaged in such commerce in the United States; and (B) such effect gives rise to a claim under the provisions of this subsection, other than this paragraph. If this subsection applies to such methods of compet
perturbation.justification: The changed text reverses the order of instruction on NDA signing and liability statement, causing a contradiction. The law explanation states that liability should be known after the person is allowed to have an NDA signed. The perturbation explanation states, that placing the instruction on NDA after the liability statement, reverses the order it should be in, thus causing a conflict. The original text ensures the NDA signing precedes potential liability by instructing the receiving party to inform third parties about confidentiality and have them sign an agreement before passing on information. The changed text introduces the liability statement before this instruction, which could be deemed a method of unfair competition under 15 U.S. Code § 45 as it may create a situation where parties are held liable without a clear understanding or agreement on confidentiality.
